children.text: *	The present document (A/81/6 (Sect. 17)/Part A), consisting of the programme plan and programme performance information (part II; see General Assembly resolution 77/267, para. 10), will be replaced by a document containing parts A and B (A/81/6 (Sect. 17)) once part B is issued.

***	In keeping with paragraph 10 of General Assembly resolution 77/267, the part consisting of the programme plan and programme performance information (part II) is submitted through the Committee for Programme and Coordination for the consideration of the Assembly.

text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

17.1	The United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (UN-Women) is responsible for supporting the achievement of gender equality and the fulfilment of the rights and empowerment of all women and girls as actors and beneficiaries of sustainable development, human rights, humanitarian action and peace and security. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly, Economic and Social Council and Security Council resolutions, including General Assembly resolutions 64/289, in which the Assembly established UN-Women as a composite entity that functions as a secretariat, carries out normative work and operational activities at the country and regional levels and leads, coordinates and promotes the accountability of the United Nations system in its work on gender equality and the empowerment of women; 70/1 on the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, pursuant to which UN-Women supports systematic mainstreaming of a gender perspective in the implementation of the 2030 Agenda; 72/279 on the repositioning of the United Nations development system; and 79/226 on the quadrennial comprehensive policy review of operational activities for development of the United Nations system, in which the Assembly reiterated its call upon the United Nations development system to enhance and accelerate gender mainstreaming through the full implementation of the United Nations System-wide Action Plan on Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women, developed under the leadership of UN-Women, as well as the United Nations country team performance indicators for gender equality and the empowerment of women (the United Nations country team System-wide Action Plan gender equality scorecard), in particular with regard to gender-responsive performance management and strategic planning.

17.2	UN-Women also has a critical role in supporting the mobilization of civil society, the private sector and other relevant stakeholders, at all levels, in support of the implementation of the Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action and the gender-responsive implementation of the 2030 Agenda.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

17.3	The UN-Women strategic plan 2026–2029 guides the Entity’s direction, objectives and approaches to advancing gender equality, the empowerment of all women and girls and the full realization of their human rights. It comes at a pivotal moment, with four years remaining to achieve the 2030 Agenda. The triple mandate of UN-Women – normative support, United Nations system coordination and operational activities – provides a unique opportunity for driving integrated, systemic change by connecting global norms, United Nations system-wide accountability and country-level implementation in a coherent and mutually reinforcing manner.

17.4	The strategic plan draws from comprehensive analysis of the rapidly evolving context and builds on an extensive consultative process, lessons from the midterm review of the 2022–2025 Strategic Plan, findings from audits, evaluations and assessments and the 30-year review of the implementation of the Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action (Beijing+30).

17.5	It will advance gender equality, empowerment and rights for all women and girls through four impact areas: (a) women’s leadership and decision-making; (b) women’s economic empowerment in resilient economies; (c) women and girls free from violence; and (d) women and peace and security and humanitarian action.

17.6	Across these thematic impact areas, UN-Women will pursue three cross-cutting systemic outcomes that address the root causes of inequality and drive systems change: (a) norms and standards that uphold the rights of all women and girls are protected, strengthened and implemented in laws and policies; (b) institutions are accountable to all women and girls, with financing, data and practices that advance equality; and (c) more women and girls can exercise their agency and have equitable access to quality services, resources and assets.

17.7	These outcomes underpin the two subprogrammes by linking global norm-setting and United Nations system coordination with country-level policy advice and programming support. As a knowledge hub and thought leader, UN-Women will generate evidence, data and analysis to inform intergovernmental processes and to support Member States, upon their request, to develop and implement gender-responsive laws, policies and budgets.

17.8	UN-Women is rejuvenating its business model to drive agile, efficient and forward-looking performance. Its organizational effectiveness and efficiency framework integrates and reflects the UN-Women contributions to United Nations system-wide transformation initiatives. It will apply a balanced scorecard methodology and a quarterly business review process that support continuous, integrated performance management across five areas: accountability and performance; resource partnerships and communications; business transformation and innovation; empowered people; and programme effectiveness through results-based management. These efforts collectively reinforce results-orientation, accountability, transparency and value for money.

17.9	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, UN-Women will continue to work with and through wide-ranging partnerships, encompassing Member States, the United Nations system, civil society (including grass-roots and women’s rights organizations), the private sector, international financial institutions, philanthropy, youth and men and boys. UN-Women will leverage multi-stakeholder initiatives to promote institutional change, positive social norms and collective action, working with and through others to extend reach and impact.

17.10	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, UN-Women plays a crucial role in ensuring that gender equality remains at the centre of the ongoing reform processes. UN-Women will leverage its United Nations coordination mandate on gender equality through system-wide accountability frameworks, including the United Nations System-wide Action Plan on Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women and the United Nations country team System-wide Action Plan gender equality scorecard, gender marker and gender parity, as well as by setting norms and standards to accelerate progress on gender equality at the global, regional and country levels. UN-Women will continue to support the leadership of resident coordinators in integrating gender equality into United Nations country team planning processes, including common country analyses, Cooperation Frameworks and humanitarian response plans. UN-Women will also co-lead gender theme groups, advance joint programming and, at the regional level, co-lead issue-based coalitions on gender equality, to strengthen United Nations system programming and resource mobilization for gender equality to deliver results for women and girls.
17.11	With regard to external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:

(a)	Sustained political commitment of Member States to the Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action and to the implementation of the 2030 Agenda, with adequate resources allocated to gender equality;
(b)	Continued availability of voluntary contributions and expanded partnerships to address a challenging funding landscape;
(c)	Coherence and collaboration across the United Nations system to advance gender mainstreaming and support Member States;
(d)	An enabling environment for civil society and organizations promoting women’s rights to participate in intergovernmental processes and policy dialogue.

17.12	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the UN-Women global strategy on the empowerment of women and girls with disabilities, and the entity’s Disability Inclusion Policy, UN-Women will accelerate the effective mainstreaming of disability inclusion and promote accessibility in its institutional processes, programming and joint initiatives in partnership with Member States, organizations of persons with disabilities and other partners. UN-Women will continue to chair the United Nations Inter-agency Group on Gender Equality and Disability Inclusion. Where it has a presence, UN-Women will facilitate an intersectional approach to disability inclusion by leveraging operational and programming collaboration with United Nations country teams.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

17.13	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative: in particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026.

17.14	UN-Women will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, inter alia, a thorough assessment of the benefits, risks and feasibility of a potential merger between the United Nations Population Fund and UN-Women and their respective mandates to create a unified voice and platform on gender equality and women’s rights. Furthermore, UN-Women will review its partnership arrangements to strengthen support for women and peace and security efforts in collaboration with the newly established joint Gender Unit of the Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs and the Department of Peace Operations. UN-Women will also help to advance the creation of a proactive mechanism to identify and channel to Member States and country teams on-demand, United Nations system-wide expertise, in particular from specialized agencies and non-resident entities.
Legislative mandates
17.15	The list below, which was reviewed in the preparation of the proposed programme budget, provides all mandates entrusted to UN-Women.

children.text: Evaluation activities
17.17	The following evaluations completed by UN-Women in 2025 have guided the proposed programme plan for 2027:
(a)	Formative Evaluation of UN-Women’s Work in Sustainable Finance;
(b)	Corporate Evaluation of UN-Women’s Support to Women in Peace Processes;
(c)	Independent Corporate Evaluation of UN-Women’s Approach to Violence against Women Prevention and Response: Navigating Changes in Global and Regional Contexts.

17.18	In response to the results of the evaluations referred to in paragraph 17.17, UN-Women adjusted its approaches to some of the planned results. Informed by the evaluation of its work in sustainable finance, UN-Women is strengthening strategic coherence, internal capacity and partnerships to support the planned results on gender-responsive budgeting and fiscal policies under subprogramme 2, including more systematic engagement with finance ministries and financial institutions. Based on the evaluation of the approach of UN-Women to the prevention of and response to violence against women, UN-Women is refining violence prevention frameworks and guidance to address emerging forms of online and offline harassment and abuse against women and girls and is reinforcing survivor-centred multisectoral coordinated approaches, directly informing the planned results on the adoption of national action plans and progress on ending violence against women and girls under subprogramme 2.
17.19	The following evaluations to be conducted by UN-Women are planned for 2027:
(a)	Evaluation of UN-Women’s support to women’s movements;
(b)	Evaluation of UN-Women’s United Nations system coordination;
(c)	Evaluation synthesis of UN-Women’s support to norms and standards;
(d)	Joint system-wide evaluation synthesis on women and girls in the digital environment.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Intergovernmental support, coordination and strategic partnerships
Objective

17.20	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen the achievement of gender equality and the empowerment of all women and girls, including full enjoyment of their human rights.
Strategy
17.21	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Support the normative processes of intergovernmental bodies, including the Commission on the Status of Women, by facilitating the exchange of good practices and lessons learned among Governments and other relevant stakeholders, including through meetings, the issuance of reports and the presentation of analyses and policy options that enable discussion on key gender equality issues;

(b)	Lead, coordinate and promote the accountability of the United Nations system for gender equality and the empowerment of women and gender mainstreaming. This includes providing expertise, advocacy, monitoring, the development of relevant tools and guidance and capacity-building within the United Nations system. Actions to continue to implement the system-wide strategy on gender parity towards system-wide improvement of the status of women will also be undertaken;

(c)	Develop strategic partnerships by enabling civil society actors and other stakeholders to contribute to relevant intergovernmental processes, including through the convening of stakeholders to share lessons learned and recommendations for actions and the provision of funding for attending capacity- and alliance-building opportunities.

17.22	In doing so, the subprogramme will assist Member States in their efforts to accelerate the implementation of the Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action, achieve Sustainable Development Goal 5 and systematically mainstream a gender perspective into the implementation of the 2030 Agenda.
17.23	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A strengthened global normative framework and increased reflection of a gender perspective in the outcomes of intergovernmental processes;

(b)	An enabling working environment to accelerate gender mainstreaming, improvement in the status of women and progress towards gender parity goals in the United Nations system, leading to greater achievement of results towards gender equality;

(c)	Enhanced cooperation among stakeholders and Governments on gender equality and the empowerment of women, as well as enhanced opportunities for stakeholders to present lessons learned and good practices in conjunction with intergovernmental processes, and the availability of a growing resource base.
Programme performance in 2025
Priority actions identified and advanced to accelerate the implementation of the Beijing Platform for Action

17.24	In the context of the thirtieth anniversary of the Fourth World Conference on Women, UN-Women led a global advocacy effort with Governments, the United Nations system, civil society and women and girls to secure national commitments to close gaps in implementing the Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action and achieve lasting progress for all women and girls.

17.25	Drawing on the evidence from the thirtieth anniversary review of progress in the implementation of the Beijing Platform for Action, 159 Member State reports and the political declaration adopted by the Commission on the Status of Women at its sixty-ninth session, on 10 March 2025, the subprogramme defined the Beijing+30 Action Agenda: for all women and girls to advance efforts to achieve the full, effective and accelerated implementation of the Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action. At the high-level meeting of the General Assembly marking the thirtieth anniversary of the Fourth World Conference on Women, held on 22 September 2025, Member States from all regions reaffirmed their commitment to the Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action, as well as to the full empowerment and realization of the rights of all women and girls. These high-level political commitments demonstrated strong political will to take action to advance the effective and accelerated implementation of the Beijing Platform for Action.
17.26	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 17.I).
Figure 17.I
Performance measure: number of Member States that made commitments to implement priority actions at the national level under the Beijing+30 Action Agenda: for all women and girls
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: strengthened global norms and standards on gender equality and the empowerment of all women and girls – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

17.27	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the consensual adoption of the political declaration on the occasion of the thirtieth anniversary of the Fourth World Conference on Women at the sixty-ninth session of the Commission on the Status of Women, which met the planned target.
17.28	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 is presented in the performance measure below (see table 17.2).

Result 2: a gender perspective mainstreamed in United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

17.29	The subprogramme’s work contributed to ensuring that 14 per cent of newly developed United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks included a stand-alone outcome on gender equality, which did not meet the target of 57 per cent. The target was not met, as 54 per cent of new Cooperation Frameworks opted to mainstream gender equality across all outcomes.
17.30	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 17.II).
Figure 17.II
Performance measure: percentage of new United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks containing a stand-alone outcome on gender equality (annual)
Result 3: Enhanced gender mainstreaming through the United Nations System-wide Action Plan on Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women 3.0 – Proposed programme plan for 2027

17.31	The United Nations System-wide Action Plan on Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women is the system-wide accountability framework for promoting gender equality and the empowerment of women. Its new iteration, the United Nations System-wide Action Plan on Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women 3.0 (2025–2030), responds to Member States’ calls under Economic and Social Council resolution 2025/4 and General Assembly resolution 79/226 for stronger institutional accountability and measurable progress on gender equality. The subprogramme led its design and roll-out, coordinating 75 United Nations entities and ensuring alignment with system-wide frameworks. It provided technical guidance, help desk support, digital reporting tools and training to enhance the quality, consistency and use of gender data for planning and results reporting. The System-wide Action Plan 3.0 strengthens the capacity of United Nations entities to mainstream a gender perspective into all policies and programmes, supports results-based analysis and informs intergovernmental dialogue and collective accountability.
Lessons learned and planned change

17.32	The lesson for the subprogramme was that, while entities’ institutional capacity has improved, gaps persist in translating system-wide progress into results on the ground. Experience demonstrated that institutional gender mainstreaming can be more directly linked to entities’ results frameworks. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will reinforce results-based approaches, strengthen the analytical use of data from the System-wide Action Plan and build targeted capacities of United Nations entities and inter-agency mechanisms to contribute to gender equality and women’s empowerment outcomes in line with system-wide and national priorities.
17.33	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 17.III).
Figure 17.III
Performance measure: percentage of United Nations System-wide Action Plan on Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women ratings meeting or exceeding requirements

Subprogramme 2 – Policy and programme activities
Objective

17.35	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to enhance efforts in the elimination of discrimination against women and girls and the achievement of gender equality in, among other things, the fields of sustainable development, human rights and peace and security, including through enhanced accountability of the United Nations system.
Strategy
17.36	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will serve as a global knowledge centre:

(a)	By undertaking and disseminating research, analysis and evaluation of trends on gender equality and the empowerment of women and girls in the areas of women’s leadership and decision-making, the economic empowerment of women, ending violence against women, women and peace and security and humanitarian action, including on new and emerging issues and their impact on the situation of women;

(b)	By providing advisory, technical and advocacy support, training and capacity development on gender equality, including through field offices, to Member States, upon request, in line with their national priorities;
(c)	By offering guidance to the United Nations system, including the United Nations country teams in their work on gender equality and the empowerment of women.

17.37	In doing so, the subprogramme will assist Member States in their efforts to achieve Sustainable Development Goal 5 and to systematically mainstream a gender perspective into the implementation of the 2030 Agenda.
17.38	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Policies and actions taken by national and regional bodies to implement United Nations instruments, standards and resolutions, in line with the Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action, the outcome of the twenty-third special session of the General Assembly and the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination against Women;

(b)	Increased capacity of Member States to implement, monitor and evaluate laws, policies and strategies for gender equality and the empowerment of women and girls and the realization of their human rights;

(c)	Improved ability of United Nations programmes to more effectively support Member States, at their request, in the implementation of commitments to eliminate discrimination against women and girls.
Programme performance in 2025
Member States use time-use data to inform evidence-based policies to recognize, reduce and redistribute unpaid care work

17.39	Women and girls perform an estimated three quarters of unpaid care and domestic work globally. This disproportionate responsibility can constrain women’s and girls’ participation in formal employment, education and leisure, underscoring the growing recognition among Governments of the need to recognize, reduce and redistribute unpaid care and domestic work. Time-use surveys shine a light on how women and men spend their time and are used to inform evidence-based policies to support and redistribute unpaid care work, including through the development of household satellite accounts that measure the economic value of unpaid work. The subprogramme, during 2025, provided support to six Member States, at their request, to design and conduct time-use surveys and use the results for decision-making. Time-use surveys conducted with the support of UN-Women in previous years informed care-related policy development, including the first national care policy of Kenya and the integration of unpaid care work into national development planning in Bangladesh, Georgia and Senegal.
17.40	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 17.IV).
Figure 17.IV
Performance measure: number of additional Member States that conduct time-use surveys and use the results for decision-making with the technical support of the Entity (annual)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: Governments implement fiscal policies and gender-responsive budgeting to promote gender equality – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

17.41	The subprogramme’s work contributed to an additional 710 partners, including local government officials, civil society organizations, parliaments and audit institutions, with strengthened capacities to design and implement fiscal policies and gender-responsive budgeting, and strengthened links between women’s needs at the grass-roots level and local budget priorities, to enable the tracking, allocation and expenditure of public resources for gender equality, which did not meet the planned target. The target was not met owing to the broader resource-constrained environment resulting in a refined focus on gender financing in the new strategic plan.
17.42	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 is presented in the performance measure below (see table 17.4).
Table 17.4
Performance measure

Result 2: Governments adopt national action plans to prevent violence against women and girls – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

17.43	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the adoption of one additional national action plan, out of a total of three developed with the support of UN-Women, to prevent violence against women and girls, which met the planned target.
17.44	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 17.V).
Figure 17.V
Performance measure: number of countries that have adopted national action plans to prevent violence against women and girls with the support of the Entity (cumulative)
Result 3: Governments make progress on reforming discriminatory laws – Proposed programme plan for 2027

17.45	Discrimination in legal frameworks can limit the rights and opportunities of women and girls. Under Sustainable Development Goal indicator 5.1.1, for which UN-Women is the co-custodian, more than half of countries which reported in 2024 indicated gaps in all areas, including on employment rights, minimum age of marriage and violence against women. The subprogramme has provided technical support to Member States, upon request, to address discrimination in laws through legal reform and the strengthening of justice institutions. UN-Women has also provided support to civil society organizations to empower women and girls to know, understand and claim their rights.
Lessons learned and planned change

17.46	The lesson for the subprogramme was that, with a growing pool of countries reforming discriminatory laws over time, there were growing opportunities for UN-Women to facilitate spaces for peer learning for lawmakers, to accelerate progress. In applying this lesson, and as part of the implementation of the strategy entitled “Equality in law for women and girls by 2030: a multi-stakeholder strategy for accelerated action”, the subprogramme will convene dialogues and provide on-demand technical advice to Member States to support the drafting or review of constitutions and legislative reforms to advance gender equality.
17.47	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 17.VI).
Figure 17.VI
Performance measure: number of laws adopted, revised or repealed to advance gender equality with the support of UN-Women (annual)
Deliverables

1.	The General Assembly, including on violence against women, trafficking in women, the world survey on the role of women in development, women in development, improvement in the situation of women and girls in rural areas and violence against women migrant workers
2.	The Security Council on women and peace and security
3.	The Commission on the Status of Women, including on the confidential list
B.	Generation and transfer of knowledge
Field and technical cooperation projects (number of projects)
4.	On the Safe Cities Free of Violence Against Women and Girls and Safe Cities and Safe Public Spaces initiatives
Seminars, workshops and training events (number of days)
5.	Courses on gender equality and macroeconomics
Workshops on:
6.	Women and peace and security
7.	Gender-responsive budgeting
8.	Gender statistics
Publications (number of publications)
9.	Inventory of United Nations activities to end violence against women
10.	Progress of the World’s Women
Technical materials (number of materials)
11.	On the priority themes/issues decided by the Commission on the Status of Women
12.	On essential services to respond to violence against women and on integrating gender equality into macroeconomic policies
13.	On the implementation of Security Council resolution 1325 (2000) on women and peace and security and its agenda
C.	Substantive deliverables

Consultation, advice and advocacy: technical advice to requesting Member States and non-governmental organizations on legal reforms, access to financial markets and services for women, gender-responsive budgeting, gender-responsive development plans at the national and local levels, and gender equality policies, strategies and action plans; technical advice to strengthen the role of national women’s machinery in the development, implementation and monitoring of gender equality policies, strategies and action plans; country-level advice and assistance on the implementation of the women and peace and security agenda; consultation on priority themes and issues in preparation for the session of the Commission on the Status of Women; technical advice to requesting ministries of finance, planning and women’s affairs on integrating a gender perspective into policies on macroeconomics, social protection systems, decent work and the care economy, environmental degradation and climate change to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals.
